radicals.meaningMnemonic: Everyone used a slide (丿) to escape the many little cages (匚) you tried to boil (灬) them in, leaving you with <radical>nothing</radical>. kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> will always be <kanji>sad</kanji>. No one chooses a life of crime if they're happy. And even if they seem like they're doing fine on the outside, deep down they're really sad.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine watching a criminal after he's done stealing from a bank. He's all alone. He stops throwing the money around. And cries. So so sad.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <kanji>sad</kanji> feelings fill the criminal's body with <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). This heat spreads and radiates out. If you walk next to them on the street, you'll feel the sad heat floating by you.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine being so sad that your entire body fills with heat. That heat even makes squiggly heat lines pop out above and around you.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If there is a <radical>drum</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> it's time to <kanji>rejoice</kanji>! And because of your mouth drum your rejoicing will be loud and booming!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine having a drum in your mouth and rejoicing about it! Imagine how loud it would be!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You loudly <kanji>rejoice</kanji> for a while when suddenly someone runs over and shoves a <reading>key</reading> (き) in your mouth, locking it shut. You can't rejoice anymore! You need that key to unlock your mouth!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine if people could just stop the rejoicing of others by locking their mouths shut with keys. What a sad world.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you take a <radical>thread</radical> and plant a <radical>rice paddy</radical> on it, the rice paddy is going to be extremely <kanji>thin</kanji>, but also <kanji>detailed</kanji>.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> can be used for <radical>comfort</radical>, but also for <kanji>medicine</kanji>. Sure, it’s a comfort to smell flowers because they smell nice, but one can also use them as medicine to heal people.

kanji.readingMnemonic: The best way to transport all the <kanji>medicine</kanji> on land is with a <reading>yak</reading> (やく). Yaks are big, strong, and very capable of transporting flower medicine like these.
kanji.readingHint: Picture a yak in your mind. Pretend you are riding atop the yak as it transports this medicine. Feel the fur of the yak in your hands as you cling on for dear life.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You're all by your<radical>self</radical> and you have to check your <radical>rice paddie</radical>s — all <radical>twenty</radical> of them! Luckily, you have an incredible <kanji>nose</kanji>, so you can quickly assess the state of your twenty rice paddies with just a few sniffs.
kanji.meaningHint: Really focus in on your nose. Your nostrils get huge, and your nose is huge. Smell the rice and its freshness in your imagination. Your nose is amazing.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you sniff your rice paddies, an unexpected smell wafts into your <kanji>nose</kanji>: <reading>花</reading>s (はな). Oh no — did you accidentally plant 花s instead of rice? Oh well, the 花s are putting your nose in such a state of bliss that you actually think things might be better this way.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>shellfish</radical> has a <radical>knife</radical> in its <kanji>side</kanji>. It's fine though, don't worry, it's just a reminder that he won the great shellfish war back when they were trying to choose the new leader.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture the shellfish leader in your mind and make sure to pay special attention to the knife that's sticking out of his side. It's right in the groove of his shell.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Because of this knife in his <kanji>side</kanji>, he can't pronounce certain words correctly. The most obvious one is when he says <reading>川</reading> (かわ), but it comes out of the side of his mouth as がわ.
kanji.readingHint: Try saying かわ out the side of your mouth like the leader of the shellfish and you'll probably say がわ too. Don't stick a knife in your side though, we won't pay your medical bills when you complain to us that this mnemonic got you injured.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical>, there's a <radical>gun</radical>. Its <radical>mouth</radical> points toward a <radical>plate</radical>. What's on the plate? Why, it's <kanji>salt</kanji>!
kanji.meaningHint: In olden days, salt was so valuable it was used as money. So imagine the gun buried in the dirt, its mouth vigilantly guarding the plate of salt.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you see the <kanji>salt</kanji>, you think to yourself, "<reading>She owe</reading>s (しお) me big time, so I'm going to go ahead and snatch her salt!" Who is she, you ask? She's the lady who owns the salt. And the gun in the dirt, of course.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>shellfish</radical> that can't live through <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>failure</kanji>. Any proper shellfish would survive through the winter, so this is quite an embarrassing <kanji>defeat</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine you're a shellfish in the cold, cold winter. The water is freezing but you must tough it out so no one will say you're a failure in spring when they find your dead, defeated shellfish body.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The best way to avoid <kanji>failure</kanji> is to say a little <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい) to distract you from the cold. In fact, this can work in any situation where you're afraid of defeat. Say a haiku aloud, it will calm your nerves and warm you from the inside.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine being a shellfish freezing in the cold winter water, but you avoid failure by muttering haiku over and over to keep you warm and determined.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> you aim your <radical>gun</radical> at from your <radical>window</radical> is an <kanji>ume</kanji>, aka a <kanji>Japanese plum</kanji>. Why? Because shooting the ume out of Japanese plum trees is, in your opinion, the best way to harvest plums.

kanji.readingMnemonic: You've probably heard of <kanji>ume</kanji> before, depending on your exposure to Japan, but an <reading>ume</reading> (うめ) is a <kanji>Japanese plum</kanji>.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is so <radical>big</radical> that it's the <kanji>cause</kanji> of all your problems.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine all the dumb things you blabber about, with your mouth open big and wide. Saying things you shouldn't say, causing all kinds of drama. Shame on you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Your big mouth is the <kanji>cause</kanji> behind all your friends and family moving to an <reading>inn</reading> (いん). They move to the inn so they don't have to live with you and hear you talk anymore.
kanji.readingHint: Who knew you could cause people to live in an inn instead of their own homes with your big mouth...

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>viking</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>towel</radical> is a totally <kanji>normal</kanji> thing. It's <kanji>ordinary</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Vikings get a lot of nasty stuff on their mouths from their messy eating and pillaging, so it makes sense that they'd all have mouth towels. It's as ordinary and normal as it gets, at least amongst the vikings.
kanji.readingMnemonic: It's <kanji>normal</kanji> for a viking to have a mouth towel, but your sloppy farmhand friend <reading>Joe</reading>(じょう) has a mouth towel too. That's a habit that's only <kanji>normal</kanji> for vikings, so why does he have one?
kanji.readingHint: When you ask Joe about his Viking mouth towel, he just tells you, "It's normal for everyone to have mouth towels. Totally ordinary." Now that you think about it, he really is a messy dude, so he probably needs it.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you can <radical>say</radical> what <radical>sound</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> makes, you must be very <kanji>discerning</kanji>. Few people are able to discern what sound a drunkard makes, but you — you can say, because you're a very <kanji>discriminating</kanji> listener.
kanji.meaningHint: Yes, you can even tell a tequila drunk from a vodka drunk based on the sounds they make. (Imagine how exactly they'd be different.) To be able to say what sound each kind of drunkard makes, it takes a very discerning, discriminating mind.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>Discerning</kanji> individuals like you are considered <reading>chic</reading> (しき). What's more chic than your <kanji>discriminating</kanji> way of listening?
kanji.readingHint: All chic people show some kind of discernment. For some it's about discerning what's fashionable. For you, it's about discriminating one kind of drunk sound from another. That's your version of chic.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical> has done an <kanji>injustice</kanji> on the world or other people. Being a criminal is a <kanji>negative</kanji> thing, and even if it was just a <kanji>mistake</kanji>, it was still a crime.
kanji.meaningHint: Think of all the criminals you know. Even if what they did was a mistake, it was still some type of injustice, right? It was something the rest of the world sees as a negative thing.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you commit an <kanji>injustice</kanji>, you'll know because <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) fills your body. Maybe you've never done any kind of big thing, but when you make a <kanji>mistake</kanji> your face fills with heat and you turn red!
kanji.readingHint: This is the heat of embarrassment, of knowing you did something negative or wrong.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>dry</radical> radical and the <kanji>dry</kanji> kanji are the same!
kanji.meaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You need to <kanji>dry</kanji> Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He hates being wet, all of the Mongols do, so your job is to dry his entire body. And his clothes, and his horse. In fact, when it rains, you have to ride next to him keeping him dry by holding an umbrella above his head.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine keeping Genghis Khan completely dry all day every day. He won't even get wet to bathe. Instead he makes you rub his body with dry sand and salts.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Should you let the <radical>sun</radical> shine on your <radical>coat rack</radical>? <kanji>Absolutely</kanji>! You should absolutely take advantage of the sun's natural freshening and deodorizing properties.
kanji.meaningHint: Pull a coat off your coat rack and give it a sniff. Don't you just love how fresh and sun-kissed it smells? You do. You absolutely do.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You're <kanji>absolutely</kanji> loving the effects of the sun on your coat rack — till a <reading>ze</reading>ppelin (ぜ) flies overhead and blocks it out. You absolutely need to get this zeppelin to move, ASAP.
kanji.readingHint: You try leaving a stern note on the zeppelin's windshield: "You absolutely cannot hover your zeppelin here. I will call the authorities, and your zeppelin will absolutely be towed."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming, and you want to <radical>walk</radical> over it, so you have to <kanji>ford</kanji> it. You don’t walk across a tsunami, you <kanji>wade</kanji> across it.
kanji.meaningHint: Imaging taking your shoes off, hiking up your pants, and holding all of your things above your head as you ford across the tsunami.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Your ability to <kanji>ford</kanji> this tsunami impresses everyone who witnesses it, especially the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). Now that you've taught him a tsunami can be forded, he's going to start sending his troops through them instead of waiting for them to pass.
kanji.readingHint: Your new fording techniques have increased the Shougun's military power! Congrats, I guess.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under this <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical> who works for the <kanji>government</kanji>. If you saw a bear out in the wild you might be afraid and try to run, but if you see a bear inside a building under a roof, don't be scared! It's just a government bear.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture the government bear looking through government documents and snorting gruffly as it decides what to sign and what to reject. It has a little government sash and hat.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The first <kanji>government</kanji> that allowed bears was set up by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He was all about conquering people and then using them as efficiently as possible. He discovered that bears are incredibly useful in government buildings as government workers.
kanji.readingHint: Picture Genghis Khan patting the first bear government worker on the back as they start their first day working for the government.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you're on the <radical>roof</radical> of the <radical>festival</radical> you'll have to <kanji>guess</kanji> what's actually going on below you. You can kind of see that there are rides and maybe food? It smells like food. But you have to guess on the specifics because you're above it and not in it.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine being on the roof above a festival. Can you guess what's happening below you based on the sounds and smells?
kanji.readingMnemonic: To get a better <kanji>guess</kanji>, you ask <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) for help. She's that little girl you know from some earlier mnemonics, and she happens to be up on the roof with you. Picture her as you ask, "What's your guess, Satsuki?"
kanji.readingHint: And what's Satsuki doing up here, anyway? Your guess is as good as mine. Now imagine you and her trying to guess what kind of festival it is.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical> your <radical>clan</radical> digs in the <radical>ground</radical>, trying to get to the <kanji>bottom</kanji>. They want to build a nice home your entire clan can live in, but they need to get to the bottom before they'll start building.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture all of the people in your clan under this canopy digging with all their might, trying to find the bottom. "Isn't there... no bottom though?" you think to yourself.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Suddenly someone down in the hole shouts that they've reached the <kanji>bottom</kanji>, but it's full of water and they're all <reading>soak</reading>ed (そこ)!
kanji.readingHint: The bottom is full of water, and everyone and everything down there is soaked through now.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Put on <radical>cleat</radical>s so you can plant a kiss on your sweetheart's <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>heart</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> — that's <kanji>love</kanji>. You'll be braving the harsh winter elements for love, so you put on your cleats to get a better grip on the snow. Sure, you could just wait for better weather to go see them, but then it wouldn't be true love, would it?
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a person you love. Feel the joy of love in your heart as you brave the winter to see them. Your cleats crunch the snow underfoot, it's cold outside, but you're kept warm and happy by that feeling of love.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Walking through the winter snow with your cleats on, you've gotta keep your <reading>eye</reading> (あい) on the prize. It's a really hard journey, but you keep your eye on the prize, which is your <kanji>love</kanji> and the time you'll get to spend with them.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine seeing your love with your own two eyes after a long time apart. It feels great to be reunited and see eye to eye once again. Imagine this feeling.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: This <radical>net</radical> is for <radical>someone</radical> who works in a <kanji>government office</kanji>. You'll also take someone from a <kanji>political office</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Doesn't matter who, but you're going to catch someone from a government office using this net, it doesn't matter who.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> that you <radical>say</radical> to <kanji>warn</kanji> people against going outside. It's cold and dangerous in winter! You <kanji>admonish</kanji> anyone and everyone you see going out there with your scary winter poem.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the things you'd warn people about. What is it about winter that's so dangerous?
kanji.readingMnemonic: To make sure people listen to your <kanji>warn</kanji>ing, you threaten to take away their <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). People will do anything for cake, so when you bring it up, they actually take your attempts to warn them about winter seriously.
kanji.readingHint:
The winter cold will make you ache
Your whole body will start to shake
Don't go outside
I'll eat your cake

kanji.meaningMnemonic:
<radical>Viking</radical> <radical>see</radical>, Viking <kanji>memorize</kanji>. That’s how Vikings learn things. They see something, and then they memorize it. Quite simple, really.

It can also mean <kanji>awake</kanji>. Just remember that Vikings can't see if they aren't awake. There won't be anything to memorize if their eyes are still closed, after all!
kanji.meaningHint: How else would a Viking memorize something? Imagine having to memorize sea charts for your Viking navigation. Look and see the maps in your brain as you memorize them.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Every morning, a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) crows and rouses the Vikings. Seeing this, one of the Vikings <kanji>memorize</kanji>s the cock crow so that he can mimic it and play pranks on his fellow Vikings.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself memorizing the sound of a cock crow and using it to play jokes on your fellow Vikings. Cock-a-doodle-doo!

rawData.meaning_hint: How else would a Viking memorize something? Imagine having to memorize sea charts for your Viking navigation. Look and see the maps in your brain as you memorize them.
rawData.reading_hint: Imagine yourself memorizing the sound of a cock crow and using it to play jokes on your fellow Vikings. Cock-a-doodle-doo!
rawData.meaning_mnemonic:
<radical>Viking</radical> <radical>see</radical>, Viking <kanji>memorize</kanji>. That’s how Vikings learn things. They see something, and then they memorize it. Quite simple, really.

It can also mean <kanji>awake</kanji>. Just remember that Vikings can't see if they aren't awake. There won't be anything to memorize if their eyes are still closed, after all!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: What do you <radical>say</radical> to a <radical>guard</radical>? Your <kanji>theory</kanji> about how the thing he's guarding isn't even real. Yeah, you have this whole thing figured out. You have for a while. At this point, it's only fair that you tell your theory to the guard who's wasting his time.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine saying your theory out loud to a guard about how what he's guarding isn't even there. Imagine how he would react.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The guard listens to your <kanji>theory</kanji> but then he says his OWN! He thinks there are two <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) of guarded items, and he's only guarding one. According to his theory, either one could be real! His theory is more plausible, don't you think?
kanji.readingHint: Which theory do you believe? That there is nothing or that there are sets of things?

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine saying your theory out loud to a guard about how what he's guarding isn't even there. Imagine how he would react.
rawData.reading_hint: Which theory do you believe? That there is nothing or that there are sets of things?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You hide <radical>poop</radical> in a <radical>cape</radical> as part of the <kanji>illusion</kanji> in your magic show. Your acts are kind of gross and this one involves creating the illusion of disappearing poop by hiding it within the folds of your cape.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine having poop in your cape. It would smell bad and anyone close to you would be able to smell right through the illusion.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Somehow the poop cape <kanji>illusion</kanji> goes over really well. The crowd is giving you a standing ovation and right in the center is <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). He marches up to you, wipes a tear from his eye, and says, "Your illusion cape is wonderful. I must have it!"
kanji.readingHint: Genji grabs the cape before you can stop him and he puts it on. You can see the illusion fade, he keeps smiling but more tears start streaming down his face. "A wonderful illusion, the best I've ever seen," he chokes out and marches away, without looking back.

He marches up to you, wipes a tear from his eye, and says, "Your illusion cape is wonderful. I must have it!"

kanji.meaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>sheep</radical> are <kanji>detailed</kanji> creatures. I mean, I never thought sheep were very detailed, but that’s what they say.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine getting really close to a sheep and seeing all the tiny detailed curls in their wool. There are hidden patterns you'd never noticed before now!
kanji.readingMnemonic: The reason these sheep are so <kanji>detailed</kanji> is that they are trying to impress the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). If they're fancy enough, perhaps the Shougun won’t kill them for their meat and make their wool into clothing.
kanji.readingHint: Picture the Shougun, leaning in close to inspect the detailed patterns all over the sheep. His nose is so close, he can probably smell all the little detailed nuances of the sheep's musk too.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> kills a <radical>pig</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> in this new <kanji>drama</kanji> about the animal kingdom! The tiger and pig used to be friends when they were younger, but now they're bitter enemies and the tiger just stabbed the pig right in the chest with a knife.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this scene from the drama as dramatically as you can. The pig stares into the tiger's eyes, "Tiger, you wouldn't–uh!" The pig's eyes slowly lower to see the knife sticking into its gut, the tiger's hand still holding it. "This is what you get, you pig," the tiger spits back, twisting.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This <kanji>drama</kanji> is being put on by <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your childhood pet gecko. Gecki is playing all the parts, because as true drama fans know, one-gecko-shows are the highest form of art.
kanji.readingHint: Picture Gecki acting out the part of the tiger and the pig in this drama. It's so intense, you're moved to tears. Bravo Gecki, bravo!

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: You know the word 外来 to mean "imported." So, combine that with the kanji for <kanji>language</kanji> and you have an imported word, aka a <vocabulary>borrowed word</vocabulary>. There are a lot of these in Japanese, which makes this word important. Almost everything in katakana is a 外来語, in fact.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.ja: リュックサックは、外来語ですか？

context_sentences.en: In fact, "ikura" is a loanword from Russia.
context_sentences.ja: 実は、「イクラ」は、ロシアからの外来語なんです。

context_sentences.en: In present day Chinese, many Japanese words are used as loanwords.
context_sentences.ja: 今の中国語では、たくさんの日本語が外来語として使われています。

context_sentences.en: In Japan, we often use foreign words. Foreign words are becoming so much a part of Japanese culture that sometimes Japanese and foreign words are difficult to tell apart.
context_sentences.ja: 日本では外来語を使う事が多いです。たまに日本語と外来語が見分けられないほど、外来語は日本文化の一部になりつつあります。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>sad</kanji> and this verb is transitive, so it means <vocabulary>to be sad about</vocabulary> something. Boohoo.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: If you're going <vocabulary>to be sad about</vocabulary> something right now, it will be <reading>kana</reading> (かな). Don't use kana when you're trying to learn kanji! You'll be sad forever if you're stuck using kana your whole life, so don't let that habit start now, friend.

context_sentences.en: I don't want to see her sad.
context_sentences.ja: 彼女が悲しむのは見たくありません。

context_sentences.en: Animals, as well as humans, are saddened by the deaths of their friends.
context_sentences.ja: 人間だけではなく、動物も仲間の死を悲しみます。

context_sentences.en: She was very saddened by the loss of her beloved pet.
context_sentences.ja: 彼女は愛するペットが亡くなってとても悲しんでいます。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>rejoice</kanji> so the verb vocab version is <vocabulary>to rejoice</vocabulary>. Though <vocabulary>to become happy</vocabulary> probably sounds a little more natural.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: You start <vocabulary>to rejoice</vocabulary> by saying, "<reading>Yo, rock on</reading> (よろこ)!" Now you know how to rejoice from now on. At church or temple? "Yo, rock on!" Perfect.

context_sentences.en: I am very happy to see my son's happy face.
context_sentences.ja: 息子の喜ぶ顔が見れてとてもうれしいです。

context_sentences.en: If even one person claims to be my fan, I will weep and rejoice!
context_sentences.ja: 私のファンだと言う人が一人でもいたら、泣いて喜びます！

context_sentences.en: I hate myself for not being able to enjoy the success of others.
context_sentences.ja: 他人の成功を喜べない自分がキライです。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
The kanji means <kanji>cure</kanji>, so this verb means <vocabulary>to cure</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to heal</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to ease</vocabulary> something. 

This verb is for when someone actively cures something. (The illness doesn't just get better on its own.) How can you remember that? Because when you try to cure someone and you mess up, they can <reading>sue</reading> (す). Basically, to cure someone is to risk getting sued.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
This word uses a kun'yomi reading you didn't learn with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic:

I need you <vocabulary>to cure</vocabulary> him, and I need you to cure him right <reading>now</reading> (なお). NOW NOW NOW!

This also shares a reading with 直す, a similar word that you already learned means "to fix." Fixing and curing are basically the same, right? Call me crazy, but there might just be an etymological connection there that can help you remember this reading.

context_sentences.en: Medications are needed to cure this disease.
context_sentences.ja: この病気を治すには薬が必要です。

context_sentences.en: Do you know how to heal scratches fast?
context_sentences.ja: キズを早く治す方法を知っていますか？

context_sentences.en: To cure a scratchy throat, it's best to tie a leek around your neck.
context_sentences.ja: ノドのイガイガを治すには、ネギを首にくくっておくのが一番です。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Gender</kanji> <kanji>love</kanji> is that kind of love that has to do with gender parts. That's <vocabulary>sexual love</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: I'm studying sexual love at university.
context_sentences.ja: 大学で、性愛学について勉強しています。

context_sentences.en: Interfering with another married couple's sexual love is just indecent.
context_sentences.ja: 他人の夫婦の性愛に口出しするなんて、下品にもほどがあるよ。

context_sentences.en: I’m not sure if I’m asexual or have erotophobia.
context_sentences.ja: 性欲が無いのか性愛恐怖症なのか自分では判断できません。
